Meta has trimmed staff across multiple units, including its Reality Labs division, as it channels resources into AI development. Oracle, Intel, and Dell Technologies have also unveiled significant workforce reductions tied to strategic realignments and cost discipline. Related News Zedcrest Group revamps leadership IEI share price jumps 700% as investors bet on recapitalisation VIDEO: Robot beats human record to win half-marathon in China Beyond macroeconomic and technological factors, layoffs are also being driven by internal recalibration. Companies are reassessing long-term bets that have failed to deliver expected returns, leading to the cancellation of projects and the elimination of associated roles. Writing on Quora, Bill McKissack argued that layoffs often follow periods of excessive optimism. вҖңTech leaders often get ahead of the market. Sometimes that pays off, but often it doesnвҖҷt,вҖқ he said, pointing to instances where companies overinvest in emerging technologies that take longer than expected to mature. Such cycles are not new. The dotcom crash of the early 2000s triggered a similar wave of layoffs after exuberant investment collided with economic reality. In many cases, companies had hired heavily based on ambitious projections that ultimately proved unrealistic. вҖңThere was a lot of excitement and over-hiring based on very optimistic projects. This was eventually followed by layoffs,вҖқ McKissack added, noting that the pattern continues to repeat as new technologies capture the industryвҖҷs imagination. Financial performance remains a central consideration. A project manager, Brant Serrxner, said the fundamental reason for layoffs is straightforward: money. Companies cut jobs when they are losing money, missing targets, or facing pressure from creditors and shareholders. вҖңThey are either losing money, not meeting planned goals, not meeting estimates made to banks and creditors, seeing stock declines, or losing business,вҖқ he said. While layoffs in the tech sector attract outsized attention, Serrxner noted that they reflect the same economic realities faced by companies in other industries. What makes the current wave particularly striking is the visibility of the tech sector and its role as a major engine of economic growth. Over the past decade, technology companies have positioned themselves as architects of the future, attracting talent with promises of stability, high pay, and long-term opportunity. The sudden reversal has therefore had a psychological impact that extends beyond the immediate job losses. At the same time, the narrative of widespread job destruction may obscure a more nuanced reality. Demand for skilled software engineers and technical talent remains strong, even as companies reduce overall headcount. Many laid-off workers, particularly those with in-demand skills, are likely to find new roles relatively quickly. вҖңMost of the good ones will have jobs before their severance runs out,вҖқ McKissack said, suggesting that layoffs are as much about recalibrating workforce quality as they are about reducing numbers. Instead, a more immediate driver of layoffs lies in decisions made during the Covid-19 pandemic. Between 2020 and 2022, tech companies embarked on an unprecedented hiring spree as lockdowns accelerated demand for digital services. Remote work made recruitment easier, while soaring revenues encouraged executives to expand aggressively. That expansion has since proven unsustainable. As growth normalised and revenues cooled, many companies found themselves with bloated payrolls. вҖңMore employees is directly proportional to higher expenses,вҖқ Bhattacharjee noted, describing the current layoffs as a necessary response to rising costs that had outpaced budgets. The broader economic environment has compounded these pressures. Higher interest rates have increased the cost of borrowing and reduced the flow of cheap capital that once fuelled tech expansion. Investors, in turn, have shifted their focus from growth at all costs to profitability and efficiency. This change in sentiment has forced companies to cut spending, particularly in areas deemed non-essential or underperforming. Entire divisions have been scaled back or shut down as executives prioritise core businesses and higher-margin activities. Recent announcements illustrate the breadth of the restructuring. Amazon has disclosed roughly 16,000 corporate job cuts in 2026, following tens of thousands of reductions in previous years. Meta has trimmed staff across multiple units, including its Reality Labs division, as it channels resources into AI development. Oracle, Intel, and Dell Technologies have also unveiled significant workforce reductions tied to strategic realignments and cost discipline. Title: Bitcoin funding rates hit most negative levels since 2023: Glassnode
URL: https://cryptobriefing.com/bitcoin-funding-rates-hit-most-negative-levels-since-2023-glassnode/
Time Published: 2026-04-16T23:08:52Z
Full Content:
Searching... Share BitcoinвҖҷs funding rates have reached their most negative levels since 2023, according to Glassnode. This suggests the potential for a local bottom, with the likelihood of Bitcoin dipping to $60,000 in April currently at 100% YES. The probability of Bitcoin being above $68,000 on April 16 sits at 99.9% YES. Historical patterns show that deeply negative funding rates have often coincided with local bottoms in past market cycles. All active sub-markets for Bitcoin price predictions in April are near certainty, with a consistent 100% YES across key dates. This includes the market predicting above $68,000 on April 16. These odds suggest traders expect Bitcoin to hold its current level through short-term volatility. Combined 24-hour trading volume across these markets is $390,585 USDC. Order book depth requires substantial capital to move prices, pointing to institutional participation. The largest price move of the day was negligible, consistent with the overall stability in market sentiment. This funding rate dip is a potential signal for traders looking for entry points. Buying YES at 99.9Вў could yield a 1.00x return if Bitcoin stays above $68,000. The historical precedent of negative funding rates preceding market bottoms could foreshadow a bullish turnaround. Watch for developments in US-Iran relations, where an extension of the ceasefire or de-escalation could further stabilize Bitcoin prices. Institutional announcements from BlackRock and Fidelity, as well as any shifts in Federal Reserve policy, are also worth tracking. Get prediction market intelligence as a structured API feed. Title: Schwab offers Bitcoin, Ether trading to retail clients amid regulatory clarity
URL: https://cryptobriefing.com/schwab-offers-bitcoin-ether-trading-to-retail-clients-amid-regulatory-clarity/
Time Published: 2026-04-16T22:48:19Z
Full Content:
Searching... Share Charles Schwab has announced plans to offer retail clients direct trading in Bitcoin and Ether. The Polymarket contract for Bitcoin dipping to $60,000 in April now sits at 0% YES. With only 14 days left in April, the odds for Bitcoin reaching $60,000 this month are effectively zero. Every active sub-market suggests Bitcoin will stay above that level. The introduction of spot trading at a major brokerage like Schwab typically supports bullish sentiment. The likelihood of Bitcoin achieving a new all-time high by June 30 is at 3.4% YES, up from 3% a week ago. The September 30 sub-market has risen more noticeably to 9.5% YES, previously 6%. The December 31 market is at 16% YES, meaning traders expect the most movement later in the year. The combined face value traded daily across these markets is $15,938. Actual USDC traded is $856, which means a few large trades could still shift odds. It takes only $1,794 to move the June market 5 points, so a single sizable order could push expectations higher. SchwabвҖҷs entry suggests confidence in recent regulatory clarity from the SEC and CFTC. At a current price above $72,000, a bet on Bitcoin falling to $60,000 in April is hard to justify. A YES share priced at 0Вў would pay $1 if Bitcoin somehow fell that far, but no obvious catalyst exists for that kind of decline. Watch for further institutional activity from major asset managers like BlackRock or Fidelity, and any geopolitical developments that could affect prices. Title: Netflix Revenues Up 16 Percent to $12.3 Billion
URL: https://www.thurrott.com/music-videos/netflix/335038/netflix-revenues-up-16-percent-to-12-3-billion
Time Published: 2026-04-16T22:33:14Z
Full Content:
Netflix reported that it earned a net income of $5.3 billion on revenues of $12.3 billion in the quarter ending March 31, 2026. Those figures represent growth of x percent and 16.2 percent, respectively, year-over-year (YOY). But they fell short of analyst expectations, triggering a 9 percent decline in the companyвҖҷs stock price. вҖңRevenue in the first quarter grew 16 percent year over year, driven primarily by membership growth, higher pricing, and increased ad revenue,вҖқ Netflix explained in a letter to shareholders. вҖңRevenue was slightly above our forecast due to higher than forecasted membership growth and favorable [currency exchange rate] movements net of hedging.вҖқ Netflix had to pay a $2.8 billion termination fee tied to its failed bid to acquire Warner Bros. during the quarter, and it recognized this cost in вҖңinterest and other income.вҖқ Despite losing out on Warner Bros., Netflix declined to adjust its revenue projects for the rest of 2026. But its estimate for the current quarter is lower than expected. The company also revealed that co-founder and chairman of the board Reed Hastings would retire from Netflix when his term expires in June after 29 years with the company. вҖңNetflix changed my life in so many ways, and my allвҖ‘time favorite memory was January 2016, when we enabled nearly the entire planet to enjoy our service,вҖқ Mr. Hastings said. вҖңMy real contribution at Netflix wasnвҖҷt a single decision; it was a focus on member joy, building a culture that others could inherit and improve, and building a company that could be both beloved by members and wildly successful for generations to come. A special thanks to Greg and Ted, whose commitment to NetflixвҖҷs greatness is so strong that I can now focus on new things.вҖқ Looking ahead, Netflix says it plans to release a new mobile experience soon that will feature a vertical video discovery feed. It also plans to continue leveraging AI to improve its recommendations for customers, and it will continue expanding into related verticals like podcasts and games. вҖңThe entertainment industry remains extraordinarily vibrant and intensely competitive,вҖқ Netflix noted. вҖңToday, our competitive set includes Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Comcast, Disney, local media companies around the world, Meta, Roblox, and TikTok, to name a few.вҖқ Paul Thurrott is an award-winning technology journalist and blogger with 30 years of industry experience and the author of 30 books. He is the owner of Thurrott.com and the host of three tech podcasts: Windows Weekly with Leo Laporte and Richard Campbell, Hands-On Windows, and First Ring Daily with Brad Sams. He was formerly the senior technology analyst at Windows IT Pro and the creator of the SuperSite for Windows from 1999 to 2014 and the Major Domo of Thurrott.com while at BWW Media Group from 2015 to 2023. You can reach Paul via email, Twitter or Mastodon. comments comment comments comment Join the crowd where the love of tech is real - become a Thurrott Premium Member today!

Title: Netflix Q1 2026 earnings beat: Why did Netflix stock fall despite strong results and weak forward guidance outlook?
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/netflix-q1-2026-earnings-beat-why-did-netflix-stock-fall-despite-strong-results-and-weak-forward-guidance-outlook/articleshow/130318036.cms
Time Published: 2026-04-16T22:04:24Z
Full Content:
Netflix (NFLX) dropped after Q1 2026 earnings despite beating profit estimates, as stock market reaction turned negative on weak outlook signals. The company posted $1.23 EPS versus $0.76 expected and $12.25B revenue, up 16% year-over-year, showing strong Netflix Q1 earnings performance. However, Netflix 2026 guidance stayed below Wall Street expectations, raising concerns about Netflix stock forecast and growth slowdown risk. Investors focused on Netflix revenue outlook $50.7BвҖ“$51.7B and margin pressure near 31.5%. The mix of strong earnings and cautious guidance created volatility. Netflix stock analysis now highlights valuation stress, insider selling, and slower future growth expectations. Title: Rocket Lab Announces Date of First Quarter 2026 Financial Results
URL: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/16/3275840/0/en/Rocket-Lab-Announces-Date-of-First-Quarter-2026-Financial-Results.html
Time Published: 2026-04-16T20:30:00Z
Full Content:
April 16, 2026 16:30 ET | Source: Rocket Lab Corporation Rocket Lab Corporation Long Beach, CA Long Beach, CA LONG BEACH, Calif., April 16, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Rocket Lab Corporation (Nasdaq: RKLB) (вҖңRocket LabвҖқ or вҖңthe CompanyвҖқ), a global leader in launch services and space systems, today announced that it will release its financial results for the first quarter 2026 following the close of the U.S. markets on Thursday May 7, 2026. Rocket Lab will host a corresponding conference call at 2:00 p.m. Pacific Time (5:00 p.m. Eastern Time). A live webcast and replay of the conference call will be available on the CompanyвҖҷs Investor Relations website at www.investors.rocketlabcorp.com. Rocket Lab Investor Relations Contact Patrick Vorenkampinvestors@rocketlabusa.com Rocket Lab Media Contact Murielle Bakermedia@rocketlabusa.com About Rocket LabRocket Lab is a leading space company that provides launch services, spacecraft, payloads, and satellite components serving commercial, government, and national security markets. Rocket LabвҖҷs Electron rocket is the worldвҖҷs most frequently launched orbital small rocket; its HASTE rocket provides hypersonic test launch capability for the U.S. government and allied nations; and its Neutron launch vehicle in development will unlock medium launch for constellation deployment, national security and exploration missions. Rocket LabвҖҷs spacecraft and satellite components have enabled more than 1,700 missions spanning commercial, defense and national security missions including GPS, constellations, and exploration missions to the Moon, Mars, and Venus. Rocket Lab is a publicly listed company on the Nasdaq stock exchange (RKLB). Learn more at www.rocketlabcorp.com. Title: It Was Once Silicon ValleyвҖҷs Favorite Shoe Brand. Now ItвҖҷs Pivoting to Something Else Entirely.
URL: https://slate.com/technology/2026/04/ai-allbirds-pivot-silicon-valley.html
Time Published: 2026-04-16T17:55:09Z
Full Content:
Enter your email to receive alerts for this author. Sign in or create an account to better manage your email preferences. Are you sure you want to unsubscribe from email alerts for Nitish Pahwa? Sign up for the Slatest to get the most insightful analysis, criticism, and advice out there, delivered to your inbox daily. On Wednesday morning, the once iconic, now struggling footwear brand Allbirds announced that it was getting out of вҖңsustainableвҖқ shoe production altogether, and transforming itself into an A.I. company. As so often happens whenever a publicly traded corporation mentions the words artificial intelligence, AllbirdsвҖҷ bottom-trailing stock earned a massive 600 percent boost upon the news. The company said it secured $50 million from an undisclosed investor to вҖңpivot its businessвҖқ from making shoes to A.I. infrastructure, according to a statement about the decision. This cash infusion will allow them to purchase graphics processing units that power and develop A.I. models, which they would then loan out to customers. As part of the shift, Allbirds also вҖңanticipatesвҖқ getting a new name: NewBird AI. All said, if you still want to relive the recent past and associate yourself with the shoe of choice for Barack Obama and Silicon Valley, you will be able to: The holdings firm that bid for Allbirds last month will license the trademark for products sold by other shoemakers and retailers. But the rest of the Allbirds (sorry, вҖңNewBird AIвҖқ) business is in a much more desperate placeвҖ”and its flailing A.I. embrace, while amusing on its face, is yet another symptom of an especially grim trend in modern-day corporate America. While the initial announcement sent AllbirdsвҖҷ stock soaring from $2.50 a share up to $17 a share, the rally had already begun settling back down as of Thursday, and the stock remains nowhere near close to the monumental high ($650) it saw during AllbirdsвҖҷ late-2021 IPO. But any surge is good news for the wool-runner manufacturer, which in March told investors it would sell all assets, at a fraction of their early-decade value, to the private holding firm American Exchange Group, owner and licensee of similarly lapsed fashion brands like Ed Hardy and Ecko Unlimited. A string of bad headlines preceded that sad agreement: Allbirds closed all its American full-price retail stores in February, and it reported massive losses throughout the past two years, thanks to a spawn of cheap imitators on the market as well as a direct-sales business model that imposed limits on how much exposure it could gain through big retail chains. (The uncertainty around President Donald TrumpвҖҷs tariffs also didnвҖҷt help, since most of AllbirdsвҖҷ products are made in Vietnam.) So while the embrace of a (for now) more lucrative industry is drastic, itвҖҷs not too surprising. As various observers have noted, this isnвҖҷt the first time a prominent brand has latched onto tech buzzwords as a means of securing investment and relevance. It was just a couple of years ago that major corporations were looking to virtual reality as the future of business, whether for internal meetings or external customer experiences. But itвҖҷs been a mighty long time since, say, Burberry has mentioned anything about its early-2020s metaverse partnershipsвҖ”and the luxury designer, whose stock is way down, laid off 1,700 people last year following steep revenue shortfalls. Albertsons also bet on interactive virtual shopping during the era of the Facebook-to-Meta vibe shift, but it didnвҖҷt exactly juice profits. Meanwhile, the Web3-crypto hype cycle that pushed clunky, glitchy headsets into every boardroom in America also brought NFTs to the foreвҖ”which has also been a complete bust. Media companies that invested in NFTs ultimately failed to earn lasting returns. Pizza brands like Papa JohnвҖҷs that bought NFTs for promotional purposes likewise failed to see their value appreciate. Reddit, which also got into the action, dismantled its NFT infrastructure at the start of the year. Shiny snake-oil bottles and product bubbles have been around for centuries. But whatвҖҷs extra notable about the sputtering tech partnerships of the 2020s is how baldly they smack of desperation. Retail outposts, brick-and-mortar food joints, and text-based media have not been thriving sectors for a while now, but theyвҖҷve been especially shellacked in the weird post-pandemic economy. Any generous cash provider, like AllbirdsвҖҷ $50 million benefactor, appears all the more enticing in those circumstances, especially if the deal trade-offs seem like theyвҖҷre cool and hip and with the times. The prospect of a hard fall afterward may not even come to mind because, hey, at least you tried. But as we get further into this decade and the economy looks ever creakier, the stakes of these boom-and-busts have become just as pressing as the perils that lead companies to take them on. ThereвҖҷs an interesting proviso in the AllbirdsвҖ“toвҖ“NewBird AI switchover thatвҖҷs gotten much less public attention. The shoemakerвҖҷs investors (who, it warrants mention, have yet to formally approve either the American Exchange sale or the A.I. pivot) are being granted a dissolution plan for the entire thing, should they decide a year from now that high-powered processing chips donвҖҷt work for them as a product line after all. Essentially, if AllbirdsвҖҷ shareholders give all of this their OK, it indicates a pretty dark ending for their once beloved footwear brand: This is the very last chance, and if NewBird AI canвҖҷt find success, it all dies out except for the mere Allbirds logo, persisting as zombie intellectual property. So the shoe that was once ubiquitous on San FranciscoвҖҷs sidewalks and praised for its open commitment to making a notoriously carbon-intensive industry just a little bit greener is turning to energy-sucking computer processors with short life spans because, well, thatвҖҷs where someone with flush pockets sensed an opening. Per federal filings, the new cash injectionвҖ”which, notably, exceeds the $39 million price tag that American Exchange will pay for AllbirdsвҖ”shall be used to buy super-efficient computing hardware from chipmakers and then sell them to A.I. enthusiasts unable to hoard or self-produce chips like larger tech firms (OpenAI, Amazon) can. ItвҖҷs a real business need, sure, and itвҖҷs not a horrible idea on the surface. But the A.I. spending surge thatвҖҷs propped up the economy over the past few years is also showing signs that it might not be as strong as previously thought, between major companies in the sector reneging on prior commitments and local communities successfully obstructing the data centers that would house the very chips a company like NewBird AI would provide. Hence the one-year timeline for a kill switch should the bubble burst sooner than the stakeholders anticipate. ItвҖҷs also a sad way for Allbirds to go out: An enterprising moneymaker swoops in on a publicly traded company thatвҖҷs shed nearly all of its onetime $4.1 billion market cap and overhauls it into something else entirely because no one involved really has any choice. This buyer, unlike the customers who once made Allbirds go viral, holds no love for the actual shoes. And just as we saw for crypto and the metaverse, plenty of other failing businesses are going to take the hasty survival fund, even if it ends in disaster. We already saw a good example in February, when the car-karaoke-machine specialist Algorhythm Holdings decided to become an A.I.-assisted freight company, giving its stock a massive 262 percent lift. Not that this lasted long: Two months later, AlgorhythmвҖҷs back to trading at one-third of that A.I. peak. If history is any indicator, AllbirdsвҖҷ last-ditch pivot will play out similarly: a company that had its feet swept out from underneath it, praying that the latest tech trend will provide a cushion before it crashes to the ground. Title: US stock market rally today: Why Dow Jones, S&P 500, and Nasdaq are suddenly rising amid ceasefire hopes and strong earnings вҖ” Is this bull run just getting started?

This US stock market rally today reflects lower geopolitical risk, stable growth outlook, and strong corporate performance, pushing equities toward record highs with sustained bullish sentiment. Title: Unstoppable surge: AMD hits all-time high in best run in nearly two decades - what's fueling the drive?
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/unstoppable-surge-amd-hits-all-time-high-in-best-run-in-nearly-two-decades-whats-fueling-the-drive/articleshow/130313216.cms
Time Published: 2026-04-16T16:50:33Z
Full Content:
AMD stock is rising very fast and hitting new highs after many days of gains. Strong demand for AI chips and servers is helping the company grow. A big deal with Meta is also boosting future hopes. Many analysts are positive, but some say the stock price is already high. Investors are now waiting for upcoming earnings results. Title: 20VC x SaaStr: вҖңI DonвҖҷt Buy Dario AnymoreвҖқ вҖ” Mythos Withheld Over Zero-Days, MetaвҖҷs Muse Spark Gets Back in the Game, SpaceX at $2T on 108x Revenue, and Why 60% Agents Are the Slow Death Spiral for Public SaaS
URL: https://www.saastr.com/20vc-x-saastr-i-dont-buy-dario-anymore-mythos-withheld-over-zero-days-metas-muse-spark-gets-back-in-the-game-spacex-at-2t-on-108x-revenue-and-why-60-agents-are-the-slow-death-sp/
Time Published: 2026-04-16T16:48:28Z
Full Content:
by Jason Lemkin | Blog Posts With Harry Stebbings, Jason Lemkin, and Rory OвҖҷDriscoll The team is back!! Anthropic unveiled Mythos and then refused to release it. Why? Because it can autonomously discover thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities, including ones sitting in code bases for years, without anyone asking it to. The market reaction: cyber stocks tumbled. Which is backwards. Every security vendor on earth is about to have to buy this capability to survive. Meta debuted Muse Spark, the first model out of Super Intelligence Labs under Alex Wang. ItвҖҷs not the best model. Not close. But itвҖҷs good enough to prove the $14 billion Scale AI acquisition wasnвҖҷt a disaster. Meta is back in the game, and theyвҖҷre pivoting toward closed source, which changes the ecosystem for everyone who built on Llama. SpaceX filed for a $2 trillion IPO on $18.5 billion of revenue. ThatвҖҷs 108x. The most expensive IPO at scale of all time. As Rory put it, вҖңThe Elon discount rate is zero and the Elon probability of failure rate is zero to get to $2 trillion.вҖқ And in the background of all of it: Jason said the thing out loud. вҖңI donвҖҷt buy Dario anymore.вҖқ Not as a CEO. As the boy who cries wolf. Meanwhile, public software stocks keep sliding because almost everyone is shipping 60% solutions that customers will not pay for. ServiceNow. HubSpot. Figma. The question every one of them has to answer: can you charge for the agent you just launched? If not, you are in a slow death spiral, and nobody on those earnings calls wants to say it. Here are the key takeaways. Mythos doesnвҖҷt just find vulnerabilities when prompted. It finds them autonomously. Point it at a code base and it surfaces zero-days on its own, including ones sitting dormant for years. Some of the Twitter counter-argument was that older models could find the same bugs if you steered them correctly. True. And totally beside the point. RoryвҖҷs framing was the cleanest in the episode: вҖңItвҖҷs the difference between a rifle and a machine gun. Both of them can kill someone. But one shoots one bullet and then you stop and reload, and the other just spews bullets out. In the First World War, we all tragically learned that machine guns areвҖҰ it might be the same thing, but quantity makes a huge difference.вҖқ The speed at which Mythos processes and reasons across large code bases means it will find more bullets. It will shoot more bullets. The Twitter cynicism that вҖңitвҖҷs not that differentвҖқ misses the quantum step in real capability. JasonвҖҷs extension: AI is enabling every single breach possible, every security hole to be found. Not a subset of the hottest companies. Not folks trying to attack OpenAI APIs. Everyone. The MyFitnessPal Cali acquisition got breached two days after close, 3.2 million records stolen, because there was no authentication on Firebase. Superbase has had to deal with the same class of issue. As more databases are built by AI, and more apps are built by AI, the number of issues is going to explode. If Mythos lets bad actors find every site the second it launches with any PII and steal it, weвҖҷre entering a transition era where security gets worse before it gets better. Security stocks fell on the Mythos reveal. The logic doesnвҖҷt hold. What Mythos actually says is that the go-forward security process will require using Anthropic or another code model to check your code before you deploy, to find these vulnerabilities before someone else does. RoryвҖҷs framing: вҖңIf the other side now have machine guns, then youвҖҷve got to build tanks. What security is may change. The vendors who step up and meet the challenge will triumph and the ones who donвҖҷt will fall away.вҖқ And the six-month delay matters. As Rory pointed out: вҖңIf Anthropic say that their model now allows anyone to find any vulnerabilities and are going to withhold it for six months, that means that in six months and one day every bad guy on the planet is going to be pinging your code and trying to find the bad bits.вҖқ ThatвҖҷs the opposite of a bear signal for cybersecurity. ThatвҖҷs every enterprise security budget getting bigger. The defenses have to match the offense. Jason said the part out loud: вҖңHe may well be the second greatest founder of all time behind Elon. Five years to $30 billion. The greatest rage startup of all time. But I am just so burned out of the boy who cries wolf. Every jobвҖҷs going to be destroyed. Everything is insecure. Enough already. IвҖҷve rotated back to Team Sam.вҖқ What specifically? The endless doom. 80% of jobs destroyed in two years. No programmers by next week. Every podcast. Every interview. Every keynote. The message stops landing after the eleventh repetition. вҖңShow me something thatвҖҷs inspiring. His message is uninspiring. ThatвҖҷs the problem.вҖқ Rory pushed back thoughtfully. The doom warnings may be wrong, but theyвҖҷre sincerely held. And thereвҖҷs value in the grandiosity. вҖңHalf of Silicon Valley is running around thinking theyвҖҷre inventing the next thing after the atom bomb. I donвҖҷt think weвҖҷre going to unemploy 50% of white collar workers. I think itвҖҷs madness. But that overwrought intensity has allowed them to build a culture where it had no churn, gave them mission clarity, and given them a $30 billion revenue line and a possibly trillion-dollar market cap.вҖқ The Mars analogy landed. вҖңThe minute we had to file an S1, someone said you might have to go to prison if you say things wrong. We said weвҖҷre not going to Mars. WeвҖҷre going to the moon. The cynical but incorrect approach would have been: I donвҖҷt think theyвҖҷre going to Mars, therefore IвҖҷm not going to do the deal. The more evolved approach is: I donвҖҷt think theyвҖҷre going to Mars, but I do think the Mars vision over 20, 30 years is a rallying cry that allows them to do amazing things in the short term.вҖқ JasonвҖҷs ask: what leaders like Dario and Sam need now isnвҖҷt more warnings. ItвҖҷs a positive vision. The Mark Andreessen deflation-and-abundance pitch. The Vinod вҖңitвҖҷs going to be okayвҖқ pitch. A little inspiration about the good. Not the fluff. Just a glimpse of what weвҖҷre building toward. HarryвҖҷs landing on it: вҖңDonвҖҷt look at what we say, look at what we do. Ignore the people ringing their hands. YouвҖҷre not buying the guilt. YouвҖҷre buying the revenue. And the revenue is amazing.вҖқ Rory closed the Dario section with the best metaphor of the episode. Founders all want their Oppenheimer moment. They want to be Vishnu, destroyer of worlds. They all reference the book, they all channel their atomic bomb. вҖңMy favorite moment in that movie was when Harry Truman says, вҖҳGet that crybaby out of the White House.вҖҷ In the end, Harry correctly says, вҖҳI dropped the bomb.вҖҷ The equivalent is if a whole bunch of people are fired, Jamie Dimon will fire them. He doesnвҖҷt need you ringing your hands with guilt, Dario. History wonвҖҷt say, Robert Oppenheimer, you killed all those people. History will say, you built the bomb, and I dropped it.вҖқ Get over the guilt. Ship the product in a methodical fashion. Be careful. But itвҖҷs not stoppable, and other people are going to own the problem. Jassy disclosed Trainium is now a $20 billion annualized business growing triple digits, with Uber as one of its biggest customers. Trainium is nearly sold out. And Mythos was largely trained on Trainium. RoryвҖҷs precise framing matters. Amazon doesnвҖҷt have a merchant silicon business competing directly with Nvidia. Amazon is not buying Nvidia chips for its own internal use, and instead uses its own chips to offer cloud hosting, inference, and training services. With a $200 billion capex budget, about half of which is chips, they buy their own where they can and Nvidia where they canвҖҷt. Anthropic running on Trainium isnвҖҷt a statement that Trainium is better. ItвҖҷs a statement that Anthropic is consuming Amazon compute, and some of that compute happens to be Trainium. But $20 billion is still $20 billion that didnвҖҷt go to Nvidia. ThatвҖҷs about 10% of NvidiaвҖҷs potential revenue. Not a mega competitor. Just an in-house bundle at meaningful scale. Nvidia may have its own Fortnite moment on a relative basis. But as Jason noted, вҖңno one is friends with his frenemies and partners better than Jensen. No oneвҖҷs played this game in the history of mankind of being kind to everybody, pulling back, being right, understanding the dynamics, and still winning.вҖқ And the Amazon-Nvidia relationship is famously difficult. Amazon is the most motivated hyperscaler to not buy from Nvidia. At the margin, 10% is material. But compute is scarce, chips are sold out, Nvidia is at $194, and weвҖҷre up against the constraint limit rather than anything else. Claude Code is directly competitive with Cursor. And now Anthropic appears to be coming for the Lovable/Replit segment with announced screenshots of a vibe-coding product. Harry said вҖңlaunched.вҖқ Jason corrected him: вҖңYou sure it was launched?вҖқ Harry: вҖңWell, they announced it.вҖқ Rory: вҖңHarryвҖҷs a media guy. He thinks when things are announced that theyвҖҷre real. Jason is a software guy. He actually thinks you have to ship product.вҖқ Eric from Bolt said everyone knew this was coming. The CTO of Lovable knew. It was just a question of when. JasonвҖҷs take on competitive threat: if this were 52 episodes ago, the answer would be вҖңitвҖҷs not important enough. TheyвҖҷre going to have to get into databases and hosting and identity management and OAuth and end-user support. ItвҖҷs a whole bunch of things culturally they donвҖҷt want to do.вҖқ But the pace at Anthropic is so intense that on a whiteboard, itвҖҷs hard not to want to grab a couple billion of extra revenue from vibe coding. ItвҖҷs 30 days of work for them. The competitive reality: they donвҖҷt have to replace Lovable or Base44 at the prosumer level. They might just go halfway and target the more technical product teams. That nerdier segment is the highest-ROI category in Replit and Lovable. Taking that slice alone is enough to maim them. And maiming hurts. This was the most important segment of the episode and Jason was the clearest on it. вҖңAlmost everybodyвҖҷs building a 60% solution. Make is an example. You look at what Claude did or prompting did last November, and you spent the last four to six months building something thatвҖҷs kind of similar to what these products were like five to six months ago. You canвҖҷt afford the tokens. YouвҖҷre worried about the cost. You canвҖҷt build all the features. YouвҖҷre trying to use cheap models to bring cost down. And you end up with Make.вҖқ HereвҖҷs the meta problem for the incumbents. A 60% solution has to be free. You have to include it with your base charge. If you could charge 60% of what Claude or Lovable or Replit charges, thatвҖҷd be great. But you canвҖҷt. A 60% product has to be given away. HubSpot launching its next group of AI agents is the perfect case. If theyвҖҷre only 60% as good as standalone solutions, HubSpot cannot charge for them. You canвҖҷt get away with charging another 20, 40, 60 grand to a HubSpot customer for an agent thatвҖҷs fine in isolation. вҖңWhen I meet with internal product teams at large companies at scale, they are so effing proud of themselves. They show me their agent. They show me their vibe coding thing. And if I didnвҖҷt use any other products, IвҖҷd think they were great too. Or if this was 51 weeks ago, I would think these products were great. TheyвҖҷre so insular at their 2,000 person company on their fancy campus. And theyвҖҷre failing even as theyвҖҷre proud of themselves, because the market will not pay. They will use your 60% solution but theyвҖҷre not going to pay for it.вҖқ ServiceNow has the biggest moat. But if its agents are only 60% as good, itвҖҷs in a slow death spiral. вҖңCheck the boxвҖқ does not work with agents. The check-the-box feature cannot be monetized in the AI era. RoryвҖҷs crystallization: вҖңUnless you have a product thatвҖҷs good enough to charge for independently, you wonвҖҷt have revenue acceleration of any meaningful scale. And if you donвҖҷt have revenue reacceleration, youвҖҷre in a different valuation bucket.вҖқ The gut-level test: can you charge for it? Yes, youвҖҷre in the growth bucket. No, youвҖҷre in the tragic value bucket. JasonвҖҷs line cut through the entire mature-SaaS debate: вҖңThe problem with moats is they keep your customers in, but they donвҖҷt lure any new ones in. No oneвҖҷs excited to cross the moat except the folks that want to breach the castle walls. The whole moat discussion is at the edge of moronic. Hooray, you signed a five-year ServiceNow deal. So what? That doesnвҖҷt bring in anybody new. It doesnвҖҷt bring in agentic revenue. It just means IвҖҷm trapped. Prisoners donвҖҷt create growth other than at the margin.вҖқ The models have radically accelerated their power since December. Opus 4.5 and whatвҖҷs coming is a step change. Anybody building to last yearвҖҷs spec slowly cannot compete. ItвҖҷs too furious. And the moat protects the prisoners while the new customers go elsewhere. Would Jason buy ServiceNow? No. WouldnвҖҷt buy any of them. Looking for products that are more than a 60% solution. The world is moving too fast. RoryвҖҷs zoom-out: вҖңThe weird thing right now is the public markets donвҖҷt have access to the growth side of software. The trade is sell SaaS, buy semis. Which effectively means sell SaaS, buy AI.вҖқ What the public markets donвҖҷt have yet is AI-native companies, starting with Anthropic and OpenAI. вҖңYouвҖҷre comparing the practical values of owning Salesforce with the mythical values of owning this company thatвҖҷs growing 10x where youвҖҷve never seen the actual financials. No oneвҖҷs seen GAAP financials, but oh my god itвҖҷs amazing.вҖқ Until five or six foundation model and AI-native companies go public, the public market cannot price this correctly. Once they do, investors can choose: Anthropic growing 5x at 30x revenue with huge losses and big SBC, or boring Salesforce growing at 10% with 30% operating margins and no SBC loss. At that point, the stocks will find their real level. Until then, public SaaS has one path up: achieve growth. Which means passing the Jason test. Build an agent customers will actually pay for. Wix spent $1.6 billion to buy back nearly 30% of its stock at $92. The stock fell 23% that week. RoryвҖҷs read: вҖңItвҖҷs kind of like an inverse Bill Gurley. Instead of selling stock and then it goes up, you buy stock and then it goes down. ThatвҖҷs like the IPO premium but the other way. Oh my god that hurts even more. Talk about leaving money on the table.вҖқ Wix did one thing brilliantly. They shipped a product (Base44) that got them beyond a 60% solution to 9-figure revenue. The argument here isnвҖҷt that the buyback was the wrong instinct. ItвҖҷs that they should have kept their capital in reserve for six or twelve months more, either to buy another AI product or invest behind their AI product. Salesforce did the bigger version. $25 billion of debt to buy back stock. On a spreadsheet it looks brilliant. In the short term, the market shrugged. RoryвҖҷs flag: вҖңOne of the big advantages you have as a public company with cash flow positive is financial flexibility. I wouldnвҖҷt trade that for anything.вҖқ If the AI wave rolls on without a blip and you donвҖҷt have a growth story, your stock will be cheap in two years. No hurry to buy it now. If there is a blip, the guy with a public currency and $25 billion in cold hard cash can buy five big things in private land. вҖңThatвҖҷs why I would have kept my $25 billion in my back pocket. You got to win the war, not the quarter.вҖқ Come to daddy, I got money. Muse Spark isnвҖҷt the best model. Reviews said itвҖҷs decent, not quite as good as the others, but good on things Scale AI was working on a year ago and weaker on newer areas the advanced labs have been developing. Makes sense. They implemented a year-ago state of the art. Rory: вҖңIf you decide as Mr. Zuckerberg that you want to be in this game, then you achieved your mission. You spent $14 billion and youвҖҷre back in the game. Now you got to move up the league table. Had you done all this and done a Llama 4 which was disappointing where people are like itвҖҷs not even credible, then youвҖҷd have felt like a fool. And you donвҖҷt.вҖқ One underappreciated shift: Meta is moving much more closed source. Llama was the American version of open source. Now theyвҖҷre pivoting. That has significant implications across the ecosystem. JasonвҖҷs strategic read: вҖңIf IвҖҷm Zuck, I donвҖҷt want to be stuck buying tokens from Anthropic or OpenAI. IвҖҷm Meta. I have the dominant consumer advertising platforms. I have the dominant social networks. I sure better own this. It turns out it is not a commodity. This is core to our existential existence just like it is for Google. And I donвҖҷt want to wither.вҖқ The scorecard on Meta big bets: $1 billion on Instagram, win $100B+ (maybe $400B). $70 billion on Reality Labs/Meta, still lose it all. $14 billion on this with a clear win. Somewhere in the middle of those outcomes is a great portfolio. And remember: a lot of folks thought AI would kill Google Search and maim Facebook. Today Google Search is a better business than it has ever been, and so are Facebook and Instagram. This is not a time to retreat. Get the lance out, go straight into battle. OpenAI is projecting $2.5 billion in ads in 2026, $11B in 2027, $25B in 2028, $53B in 2029. The pilot hit $100 million annualized in six weeks with 600 advertisers. RoryвҖҷs math: вҖңThe funny thing is IвҖҷm mentally giving them 100% credit for getting that. OpenAI is typically not unaggressive in its projections, so letвҖҷs assume this is progressively aggressive. This is going to sound really awful, but $100 billion is amazing. ItвҖҷs not enough relative to the market cap. In the context of a total trillion dollars of ads with Meta at 300, Google at 200, Amazon at 100, and TV has to eat too, thatвҖҷs a realistic high-end estimate. You need another hundred billion plus from your enterprise business. Consumer alone ainвҖҷt going to be enough to feed this beast, because your competitor whoвҖҷs all in on enterprise is already at 30.вҖқ JasonвҖҷs take on execution: вҖңThis is our job. Every week weвҖҷre going to iterate. WeвҖҷre going to improve it. It is mathematically possible. ItвҖҷs not as aspirational as the enterprise stuff. This is doable. And if it comes up short a year or two like Elon, it would suck for the IPO, but itвҖҷs not fatal. Because it is achievable, it will be achieved.вҖқ Denise Dresser (President and CRO at OpenAI, formerly CEO of Slack) leaked a memo this week claiming Anthropic is overstating revenue and that OpenAI has capacity while Anthropic is out of it. JasonвҖҷs first read: вҖңIt seemed like a flashback to something Marc Benioff might write. More appropriate for Salesforce than for OpenAI.вҖқ But on reflection: вҖңThis is the exact type of messaging you want to win traditional enterprise customers. IвҖҷm pretty bullish on OpenAI in the enterprise because all this enterprise DNA they have, which probably didnвҖҷt help a snailвҖҷs inch the last 12 months, is going to matter more and more when the models are so powerful and big enterprises are making decisions between a couple of top brands.вҖқ Last yearвҖҷs playbook was вҖңask your developer.вҖқ Anthropic won that game. Developers picked Opus for their apps, and the revenue followed. Classic Twilio mantra. It worked until it didnвҖҷt. Going forward: OpenAI doubling in size with enterprise selling motions will work better and better in 2027. The traditional playbook wins when you go deep into the Fortune 2000. Aaron Levie had a tweet this week about his road show meetings with CIOs that both Jason and Rory flagged as must-reads. CIOs are token-maxing: creating fixed dollar budgets for AI tokens for the coming year and making departments fight it out per project. Jason: вҖңThis is where the game is going to change again. When most of the budget is essentially rogue, when itвҖҷs developer teams picking Anthropic almost universally last year, thatвҖҷs one thing. When the CIO takes control again of how many tokens across a large enterprise, thatвҖҷs a very different calculus of which vendor I choose. And if the CIO prefers to buy OpenAI because itвҖҷs got a more traditional sales motion and itвҖҷs packaged better, then the enterprise is standardizing on OpenAI for 2028.вҖқ RoryвҖҷs implication: вҖңIf you are correct, the people who should go into guidance counseling are Microsoft and OpenAI, because they need to get their relationship back together again. Microsoft is the dominant path to every single enterprise in the world. They need couples therapy. ThatвҖҷs an indulgence OpenAI can no longer afford.вҖқ Microsoft can go top down. The other guys stole the march on developer love. If enterprise is two-thirds of the game, Microsoft is the key, and OpenAI plus Microsoft aligned is a very hard combination to beat. RoryвҖҷs biggest aha of the episode. Historically, consumer has been the bigger half. GoogleвҖҷs consumer business and ads is two-thirds of the value, cloud is one-third. Facebook is pure consumer. The big money has been in consumer. вҖңBut consumers fundamentally when I go home want to buy Netflix. When I go to work I want to buy intelligence. It may well be that enterprise is two-thirds of the ball game in AI and consumer is one-third or less. Which is the flip of the last time. Three years ago when ChatGPT exploded, you would have thought consumer was the launching point. But if enterprise is the better place, then yeah, youвҖҷre going to have a good consumer business, but the ball game from a customerвҖҷs perspective is two-thirds enterprise, one-third consumer. Which is the mirror opposite of the internet.вҖқ This ties to the persona question. Consumers want supportive answers. Enterprises want harsh critique and decisions. Research already shows younger users prefer OpenAI because itвҖҷs more emotionally supportive. In business, the opposite is true. Investors donвҖҷt want support. They want вҖңthis is a stupid deal.вҖқ Whether the same underlying model can serve both is an open question. The tuning for each may need to diverge. Rory on Aaron: вҖңHeвҖҷs in that rare combination of grounded enough in terms of 15 years calling on CIOs to really know what they think, and at the same time young enough and flexible enough to really understand what AI is doing. ItвҖҷs literally like an investment insight. My colleague even said after he spoke at our annual meeting: it was like an investment memo spewed out on enterprise AI.вҖқ JasonвҖҷs edge on it: вҖңIf he canвҖҷt reaccelerate Box with his incredible depth of like 10 out of 10, what hope is there for so many other leaders, so many other unicorns? If he canвҖҷt get Box to 20 to 30% growth, IвҖҷm giving up on the rest of the world.вҖқ If the best and most insightful enterprise software CEO in the game canвҖҷt pull his own company back to high growth, the prognosis for the dozens of mid-cap SaaS companies behind him is grim. $18.5 billion in revenue. $5 billion loss, mostly from xAI acquisition accounting. $2 trillion target valuation. ThatвҖҷs 108x revenue. No company has ever gone public at that multiple at this scale. RoryвҖҷs caveat on the accounting: вҖңPooled accounting doesnвҖҷt exist anymore where you retroactively recast the financials as if the companies were together. So thatвҖҷs only the loss from when xAI was acquired. We actually donвҖҷt know the actual run rate.вҖқ And then the valuation frame: вҖңThe case will obviously be all the future things. You take these adjacent TAMs. If you give them a 100% probability of happening and a 100% probability of them happening right now with no NPV because it takes five years to make it happen, then you probably get to $2 trillion. If on the other hand, you apply a probability of it not happening and a time value of money, you get to a lower number. The Elon believers are saying, these are the future things, and I ascribe 100% probability of success. ItвҖҷs basically the Elon discount rate. The Elon discount rate is zero. And the Elon probability of failure rate is zero to get to $2 trillion.вҖқ The existing business is real. A near-monopoly on cost-effective launch, with prices potentially coming down with the next-generation rocket. Starlink is an amazing business. The xAI $250 billion acquisition is harder to defend, but itвҖҷs done. The IPO will print. The question is what the multiple looks like 18 months after pricing when the new initiatives (direct-to-cellular, data centers in space) either prove out or donвҖҷt. AppLovin: $4.5 million in revenue per employee with 898 people. Rory on the caveat: вҖңRevenue per employee is not a useful metric as a one-shot-fits-all. Cursor does $4 million per employee and Salesforce does $700K. Well, Salesforce has 30% operating margins and Cursor is losing a lot of money because they spend a whole ton on tokens. You can compare companies in the same business on efficiency, but one shot fits all doesnвҖҷt cut it.вҖқ That said, JasonвҖҷs directional point is correct: everyone wants to be small by choice. Not because the economics are forced, but because leaner is better. AI is an enabler. It lets your best engineers do more. It lets you get rid of SDRs. The core test Harry tweeted: knowing what you now know about a person, would you hire them again? JasonвҖҷs version: would you rather replace them with an agent? вҖңIвҖҷd rather have an agent than a mediocre person. Everyone thinks that. Everyone. Not everyone says it out loud.вҖқ In 18 months, everyone will know how to build a good agent. Prompt engineers went from hot to irrelevant in 24 months. Agent engineering is on the same trajectory. The skill isnвҖҷt building the agent. ItвҖҷs deciding which humans to replace with one. RoryвҖҷs reformulation as a metric: вҖңItвҖҷs not fair to say to a SaaS company, hey, AppLovin does $4.5 million per head and yours does $500K. But what is fair: last year you were at $500K. This year you better be at $600K because JasonвҖҷs telling you. And next year maybe you got to be at $800K. If youвҖҷre not making progress on that metric as a software company, you are not with the program.вҖқ Thoma Bravo shutting down its growth equity arm matters. Their core playbook: buy control positions in software companies with leverage, add on other companies, sell to another PE buyer. ThatвҖҷs 90% of the money. Growth equity (non-control, minority positions in late-stage high-growth companies) is a different business. Rory: вҖңIn a period like right now where your core business is threatened, the first rule of threaten is you retreat to the core. PE guys regularly come into growth venture at the top of markets thinking this looks easy and regularly retreat when they discover itвҖҷs hard.вҖқ On the underlying private companies (Coupa, Anaplan, Medallia): these are mature vanilla SaaS at single-digit growth. Public equivalents trade at 2-4x revenue. If you bought at 10x with leverage, the math is terrifying. Apply public market comps and deduct debt, you get little or no enterprise value. Big losses in some PE funds are very possible. The counter-argument: private ownership means acute clarity. Replace management. Buy AI assets. Bolt them on. Make the transformation to AI-first that public markets wonвҖҷt let you make. If you can add a 100% agent that customers will pay for, you earn your carry. If you canвҖҷt, and the underlying software markets donвҖҷt bounce back, itвҖҷs a train wreck. JasonвҖҷs tragedy framing: вҖңIf you have 10,000 or 50,000 or 100,000 reasonably happy customers, and youвҖҷve had 15 months to build them an agentic product with access to the LLMs and 50 of your best engineers, and you didnвҖҷt take advantage of your installed base for real, this is a missed opportunity. ItвҖҷs tragic. Most teams are so mediocre and so paralyzed. People are asking me what they should do with their agents. They should be showing me their agents and asking for constructive criticism. People are paralyzed with fear. They donвҖҷt want to work twice as hard as they used to and they donвҖҷt know what to build.вҖқ Adobe-era lesson from Jason: when he was a VP at Adobe during the cloud transition, there were 100 surplus amazing engineers. By design or by accident. Sometimes on the back half of their career. Not as raised as the top Replit or Cursor engineers. But great. ItвҖҷs a crying shame you canвҖҷt take a team of six at Coupa or Anaplan, build the worldвҖҷs best agent, and ship it to your 50,000 customers. The base is there. The opportunity is there. Stebbings/Driscoll/Lemkin AI consulting ($2M a year, checkered shirts, checkered ties, coming in to teach you how to get to a 60% solution by year-end) is not going to get them there. JasonвҖҷs call: SpaceX first (already filed), then Anthropic (just added the Novartis CEO to the board, classic pre-IPO move, along with finding an audit committee chair), then OpenAI. Will Sarah Friar be CFO of OpenAI when they IPO? RoryвҖҷs frame: вҖңCEOs and CFOs have to be wildly aligned, and the CFO should probably report to the CEO. Right now the CFO reports to the president VJ, which seems anomalous. Rather than doing yes-or-no on whether sheвҖҷs in or out, what IвҖҷd say is: the IPO is going to happen, this team is going to make it happen, they need absolute sync and probably a more traditional reporting structure. Stop the leaking, stay in sync. Those are going to be the two people on the road show. They should be able to finish each otherвҖҷs sentences. You should be able to put them in separate rooms like a police interrogation and each should say exactly the same thing.вҖқ JasonвҖҷs nuance: thereвҖҷs often a case for islands of stability in your management team even if theyвҖҷre not perfect. Every turnover takes a toll. вҖңSarah is so experienced that things work. Workday finally works. We finally got all these things to work. I got 99 problems. This is not one I want to tackle.вҖқ But the daylight cannot be public. If an executive is truly arguing with the CEO in the media, or pulling a Dario-circa-OpenAI move of going directly to the board with вҖңIвҖҷll only stay if I report to the board,вҖқ they have to go. No matter how good they are. And JasonвҖҷs advice to any VP who gets this far on the pod: вҖңWhatever you do, do not reach out to your VCs to say thereвҖҷs problems with your CEO. YouвҖҷre losing your job. And it doesnвҖҷt matter if youвҖҷre right. What are they going to do, fire the CEO over you? Zero. Unless thereвҖҷs fraud, zero. YouвҖҷre gone. Maybe in three months, maybe that afternoon. Just resign. Resign with grace.вҖқ The market is splitting into two buckets. AI-native companies that canвҖҷt go public fast enough. And legacy software companies getting repriced against a backdrop of 60% solutions and flat growth. The public markets know this. The trade right now is sell SaaS, buy semis. ItвҖҷs a proxy for buying AI exposure when you canвҖҷt buy the AI-native companies directly. Mythos is a preview. Anthropic built something so capable it felt irresponsible to ship. That capability will exist in the wild within 12 months regardless. Every software vendor needs to plan for a world where AI can break and fix everything, and where the only moat is velocity. SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI will be three of the largest IPOs in history. And every public B2B company trading below 5x revenue is either a value trap or a comeback story in waiting. Which one depends entirely on whether they can build an agent customers will actually pay for. Speed. Ship. Charge for what you build. If you canвҖҷt charge for it, you donвҖҷt have a product. You have a line item. вҖңI donвҖҷt buy DarioвҖҷs endless doom anymore. He may well be the second greatest founder of all time behind Elon. Five years to $30 billion. The greatest rage startup of all time. But I am just so burned out of the boy who cries wolf. Every jobвҖҷs going to be destroyed. Everything is insecure. Enough already. IвҖҷve rotated back to Team Sam.вҖқ вҖңWhen I meet with internal product teams at large companies at scale, they are so effing proud of themselves. They show me their agent. If I didnвҖҷt use any other products, IвҖҷd think they were great too. Or if this was 51 weeks ago. TheyвҖҷre so insular at their 2,000 person company on their fancy campus. And theyвҖҷre failing even as theyвҖҷre proud of themselves, because the market will not pay for a 60% solution.вҖқ вҖңThe problem with moats is they keep your customers in, but they donвҖҷt lure any new ones in. No oneвҖҷs excited to cross the moat except the folks that want to breach the castle walls. Prisoners donвҖҷt create growth.вҖқ вҖңHarry, youвҖҷre a media guy. You think when things are announced that theyвҖҷre real. Jason is a software guy. He actually thinks you have to ship product. No, itвҖҷs all about the announcement. Surely youвҖҷve seen that in the last few days.вҖқ вҖңJason, you said heвҖҷs not inspiring enough. What would make you happy? What do you think he should say?вҖқ вҖңArenвҖҷt you loving this, Rory? Jason is like, 10% is material. We donвҖҷt need to spend all over 10% is material, right?вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs the difference between a rifle and a machine gun. Both of them can kill someone. But one shoots one bullet and then you stop and reload, and the other just spews bullets out. In the First World War, we all tragically learned that machine guns areвҖҰ it might be the same thing, but quantity makes a huge difference.вҖқ вҖңCan you charge for it? ThatвҖҷs the test. If yes, then youвҖҷre on the increasing value scale. If no, then youвҖҷre in the tragic value bucket, doing hard things that would make Dario and Sam cry. SBC reduction. Headcount reduction. At best you build a mini version of IBM/CA. Nothing magical will ever happen to a high single-digit growth tech company thatвҖҷs kicking off cash.вҖқ вҖңThe Elon discount rate is zero and the Elon probability of failure rate is zero to get to $2 trillion. If you put a more conservative number in both of those, you probably end up in a different place. You still have the upside. But are you getting paid for the risk?вҖқ This post is part of the ongoing 20VC x SaaStr collaboration with Harry Stebbings and Rory OвҖҷDriscoll.
